sentences.text: My name is Leopold, L-E-O-P-O-L-D, Zylberman, Z-Y-L-B-E-R-M-A-N.
I'm known with my friend by my nickname, Lutek.
L-U-T-E-K.
80 and a-- 82 and a bit.
I was born in Warsaw, W-A-R-S-O-W, in Poland.
Yes.
You see, I grew-- was born during the war.
While my father was a refugee in Russia, my mother that changed to another flat.
That means I don't remember my place I was born.
But it was a big for, for me, very huge rooms and a huge kitchen.
You see, there were, at that time, there were six of us.
There were the grown-ups and the, and the kids.
You see, it was my father's second marriage.
And from his first wife, there were three big kids and from my mother, there were three small kids, you know.
What I remember, just like a dream, that I always wanted to grow up because it was ration time.
And there was different food for the kids.
If we managed to get some butter or white sugar, it was for the kid.
And milk, white tea or tea with the milk was for the grown-ups.
Now, you see, the place we were was a flat in the Jewish district.
I was-- Jewish districts, you see, different districts in Warsaw, but a lot of Poles lived in a Jewish district.
A lot of Jews lived in a Polish district.
And this was a mixed neighborhood.
The address was street Murnowska.
M-U-R-N-O-W-S-K-A.
That's the name of the street, 24.
The flat was 39.
Oh, the place where I grew up.
You see, that was a big block of flats built just like they built in Europe, every block of flat adjoining the other one.
That means our block was [INAUDIBLE] two streets from the [INAUDIBLE].
One was to Murnowska and one was to Niska, N-I-S-K-A.
And we live in the second frontage, Niska.
Now, the flats in the frontage were occupied by the rich families and the flats on the side by the poor families and all depend on store.
On the attic, there lived a few Christian people, widows, et cetera, because there was all the sort of one room flat, and nothing like, you see, we see now, very primitive.
You see, ours was a Polish-speaking family, because my older siblings, they were at high school, and so on.
And that was an idea that, you see, while we go, while we grow up, we should know Polish so no discrimination at school, et cetera, et cetera.
And say, at that time, among neighborhood what I have [INAUDIBLE] talking that they have to speak Polish to take a place in society.
And Yiddish, they will pick up.
Not, not much, not unless you see some grandparents or relatives from the country came.
My Yiddish was good enough, you see.
But just the same, I could communicate.
You see, the parents spoke Yiddish is wanted to keep secrets from us.
But we understood every word.
And at first, on account of our Polish, to a certain period, we were not allowed to play in the courtyard, you see, with the kids, until our Polish was good enough so nothing can damage it.
Of course, a little bit of Yiddish you learned, because they brought in a teacher to of prayers, et cetera.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] you see, life was going on.
We didn't, in Warsaw so over the period, we didn't feel what they say today the state business.
Because for me, the state business is sort of like they coming, came after the war.
The smaller countryside place you came, the more, the bigger aristocrat you were.
About Warsaw.
About the, about the attitude that we wanted, well, that we have friends, Poles, you see, that meets in the courtyard, on the street.
And you see, we were not sort of discriminated, you see.
Just there was jokes, you see, stupid Jews.
Stupid think.
You killed our Lord Christus, et cetera.
But it was more like a joke, you see.
We did not feel anti-Semitism.
Now, you see, I go a little bit ahead.
You see, my older sister, you see, which was born in 1901.
Her name was Luzia.
L-U-Z-I-A, which confirms straws.
During the war, you see, that means actually, they-- my father married first a religious woman.
And my, when she died and my mother came to the household, she converted it to more progressive, because she was generations way back from the big city.
During the war, I don't know how much schooling she done.
But I think she was intelligent enough.
And during the war--
First World War.
She was sent to an embroidery school, you see, which came handy later in life.
My second brother, who was born 1903, he was at high school.
And my third brother--
Henry, you see.
H-E-N-R-Y.
You see, he had a Yiddish name, but that's outside the question.
Now, the other brother was Mietek.
Actually, his Jewish name was Mordechai.
Mietek, M-I-E-T-E-K.
You see, that means, Mietek, you see later, I think he was a [INAUDIBLE] engineering.
And he committed suicide.
But this is later in, in life.
And Then the second, the little children was Minia.
She was two years my se-- three years my senior, and Rubin, who was one year my senior, and myself.
Minia, M-I-N-I.
That's what we called her.
I don't know what was written, the birth certificate.
And Rubin was actually Rubin, R-U-B-I-N.
And he was, he was a year older than I.
And we sort of have been growing up like a closed up, you see, triplets or something, one for all, all for one.
Now, we had no idea that there was a mother before our mother came, because the conditions were, they were perfect, you see.
They adored my mother.
You see, this was concerns my oldest brother, it is up to a time when he got married and brought in his wife.
And wife, as usual, you see, they all want to steal the affection from their mothers-in-law.
And then slowly we're grown up enough, you see, that when we visiting friends and ask what's your name, [INAUDIBLE] you're Abram's son.
You must be from the second wife.
And we came, we found out the story, you see, from other people, from strangers.
Abram.
A-B-R-A-M.
Mother was Tauba, T-A-U-B-A.
My father was sort of a merchant warehouse man with-- he was selling men's accessories like braces, belts, gaiters, garters, and all sorts of thing.
He came from a small township some hundred kilometers, you know.
It's called Melaway.
M-E-L-A-W-A, you see, and he was telling us a story when the township was burnt.
That was a big thing.
So he left the shiva and went to Warsaw to learn a trade or get an apprenticeship.
Now, he finished a big businessman, you see, because Warsaw was exporting everything to Russia.
You see, they had all the connections in Russia.
And you see, when the war broke out, the First World War in 1914, the ban before the Russian [INAUDIBLE] were Warsaw or something.
The men were told to escape together with army.
That repeated itself in the second.
And actually, he went with the army because he had big connections in Moscow.
And he had customers who owed him money, you see.
He wasn't so much patriotic to Tsar Nikolai.
And we sort of lived in harmony, quarrel, et cetera.
Well, there were fights.
The wives, the youngest are being pushed around.
And I look forwards to getting older.
His own business.
No, no, Abram Zylberman.
It was the, you see, that [INAUDIBLE] I remember the pre-war stationery that was in Russian and in Polish, you know.
And--
The business address was in, very near to our home in [POLISH], you see.
That means it was at the same building where we first lived before we moved.
[POLISH] It's Franciscan, you can say.
And so that's sort of where I grew up.
Yeah, let me-- you see that, you see that means it was the average, middle class, Jewish, Warsaw family that, you see, dinner actually was at lunch.
And there was a snack.
And after lunch, father was lying on the ottoman, you see.
This is what we found out now, it's called the siesta.
And during the vacation, you see, there was very 30-mile long pine forest with a little sort of holiday places.
And we used to, we used to, families used to take the cases and the, from the farmer also.
He used to come, say, one day 6:00 in the morning, pick up all the things.
And we took a cab to the airport to-- I'm sorry, to the, to the railway station and came over.
By that time, it was four or five o'clock.
He already came with his wagon, horse wagon, with the things and were settling our sales.
And the kids were collecting kindling and pines, chasing squirrels.
It was a big sort of entertainment, going out to the railway track and counting the track, the cars, and the freight were railways.
And you see, this, they used to do it year after year after year.

But the one I remember was in, must have been in 1920, you know, because you see, we were watching the telegraph wires and were waiting until a telegram comes through, which never comes, only sort of birds perching.
And news came that Trotzky is coming.
Oh, I didn't know who Trotsky was.
But you see, some people say he is a Jew.
And we thought that it was sort of kind of a devil.
And you see, that means we gave the farmer, you know, to put on the stuff on the wagon.
And we came, we went to Warsaw by the narrow gauge railway, because the wide railway was carrying troops, et cetera, et cetera, which I found out later.
At that time, I wouldn't know why.
But I just take it for granted.
We came to east Warsaw, which was called Praga.
And across the bridge, you see, carrying that little parcels.
And well, the five-- that means the three of us and mother, because the kid, all the kids were already in Warsaw, for they went a few days later.
And we took a horse cab who came to the courtyard, you see.
And nothing was sort of changed.
There was a street musician.
And the, the hit of the day was a song which I recognized later, 'Under the Bridges of Paris,' you know.
And we came to the second frontage up to third floor.
And there was my father and my oldest brother and my two uncles.
And they had blisters on their hand, because they were pick up to dig trenches.
That was 1920.
They were fancy for Trotsky, you see.
And you see, they had blisters and so on.
And we found out that they went out digging trenches.
And there was a police precinct on the Niska Street.
And we saw, you see, they were picking up people from homes and taking them out of town.
You see, other than that, there was a priest, Father Skolrupka, who--
S-K-O-L-R-U-P-K-A, who went across the front with a big cross and Christ, you see, to fight the Bolsheviks.
And he was perforated with the bullets, you see, and became a national hero, you know.
And they were sort of very, very nervous days.
And one day, Henry came up.
And they said, well, they, they signed the peace.
Well, the peace, the word peace in Polish corresponds to the word room in Polish, you see.
That means room.
So I couldn't sort of make up whether to sign a room or a chamber or something like this.
But slowly I was explained.
And everybody was happy.
And soon enough, the boys came back from [POLISH].
I don't know whether they heard about it.
You see, when the Trotsky's offensive was going on, a lot of Jewish boys were in the army, you see, volunteers or conscripts.
And that was in '20.
And high commander, high brass was, afraid that they are [INAUDIBLE] for Trotsky, because a Communist and Trotsky was a Jew.
You see, didn't, he didn't make us any good.
So you see, they put them in a camp.
Yes, we knew a few brothers of my uncle, my auntie.
So you see, that means two brothers were and a few neighbors, you see.
I could remember.
And you see, that means they put them in the camp in case they have to withdraw from the front.
They said that they camps will be burnt, you know, which anyway.
They came back.

And they were the-- the hit of the day, where, was the song, 'We are the [POLISH]' And the hope is to get burnt on the stakes, you see, because the intention was that if the Polish army had to move back, you see, this camp had to be put afire.
I don't know what was the intention, but that was sort of this song that everybody was singing, you see.
They really--
Pardon?
Yes.
It was the-- it was the tune of the Polish first brigade.
But the lyrics were, we the [POLISH], the hope is the fire or stake.
You see, it's hard to translate, you see, [POLISH]
Yes.
[SINGING IN POLISH] So [POLISH] means stake, you see, the stake when the burn [INAUDIBLE].
I was, I should have been sort of five, because this was autumn, 1920.
I was born in January.
That means why I was going for six.
Everything was fair.
And slowly, you see, people were coming from the country.
And food was available and everything.
There was still-- there was bread on ration bread.
And there was black bread, which was white, you know.
And, and then was a problem you see.
Rubin was due for school, you see.
He was six and a half or something or [INAUDIBLE] seven.
They didn't know what to do with me.
We went to a school, Tarbut.
That was a Jewish school, you see.
There was an organization run by the left wing of the Zionist party which managed about five or six schools in Warsaw, you see, sort of in preparation to go to Israel or something.
My mother didn't understand them well enough, you see, because they say, why should they go to a, a Polish school or to a, a state school, primary school, and have a rabbi at home?
Now, they're going to learn Hebrew.
Well, Mr.
Katz, Katz, Mr.
Katz.
K-A-T-Z, you see, and he made us an examine, how much is 1,000 and another 1,000, you see.
So it is just a sort of stupid thing.
And he said, all right, the missus says, we will get the young boy, too.
Anyway--
At the Tarbut, we were taught everything in Hebrew, other than the Polish and Polish history, which was in the, in Polish, you see.
And, and we went.
And that was sort of the first language at school.
You know, now speaking about conditions in Poland, you see, the Jews were not strangers.
They were a part of Poland, you see, just like you say this place was frequented by nobles, peasants, and Jews.
You see, this was a part of the nation.
And they had to be mixed up, because you see, there were special, some Jewish trades and Polish trade.
The Jews were tailors and watch makers or something like this.
So, so there was an exchange in business.
